content.full_text:
RAVI MEHTA
CHAIRMAN

FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION

December 4, 1995


Kathryn A. Lynch
Lynch & Associates
1127 11th Street, Suite 452
Sacramento, CA 95814

Re: Your Request for Informal Assistance
    Our File No. I-95-335

Dear Ms. Lynch:

     This is in response to your letter requesting advice
regarding the lobbying disclosure provisions of the Political
Reform Act (the "Act"). 1/ Since you have not identified the
client on whose behalf you are requesting advice, we are treating
your request as one for informal assistance. 2/

QUESTION

     A lobbyist employer made a contribution in the third quarter
of 1995. However, the contribution was never received by the
recipient and a new check was issued in the fourth quarter of
1995. Does the lobbyist employer disclose the contribution on its
third or fourth quarter disclosure form?

CONCLUSION

     The lobbyist employer should disclose the contribution on its
disclosure statement covering the fourth quarter of 1995.


1/     Government Code Sections 81000-91015. All statutory
references are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated.
Commission regulations appear at 2 California Code of Regulations
Section 18000, et seq. All references to regulations are to Title
2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations.

2/     Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the
immunity provided by an opinion or formal written advice.
(Government Code Section 83114; 2 Cal. Code of Regs. Section
18329(c)(3).)


428 J STREET, SUITE 800, SACRAMENTO, CALIFORNIA 95814 (916) 322-5660

Our File No. I-95-335
Page 2


FACTS

A client of your firm sent a check on August 29, 1995, to a
legislator's campaign office for attendance at the legislator's
fundraising event. The check was never received by the
legislator's office and the check was returned to your client's office and
subsequently lost. A new check was issued in the fourth quarter
of 1995.

ANALYSIS

Lobbyist employers are required to file quarterly reports
disclosing contributions of one hundred dollars ($100) or more
made to legislators. (Section 86116.) Since the contribution
made in the fourth quarter of 1995 was received by the legislator,
the lobbyist employer should only identify that contribution on
its quarterly disclosure report. It is recommended that a
notation be included in the records for the contribution made on
August 29, 1995, was never received and subsequently lost.

If you have questions concerning this letter, please contact
me at (916) 322-5660.

Sincerely,


Steven G. Churchwell
General Counsel

by: Lynda Cassady
Assistant Division Chief
Technical Assistance Division
